events.text: NUTA's main control room was quiet.

Not silent—the paradoxical quiet of all machinery running at full speed, all systems at maximum load. Those sounds were so uniform, so continuous, they merged into white noise that absorbed all other sound, making the whole space feel completely isolated from the outside world.
Soren stood at the main terminal.

events.speaker: Soren
events.text: You came. I thought you'd be later, Lyra.

events.speaker: Lyra
events.text: I walked faster than you thought I would. Soren, step away from that terminal.

events.speaker: Soren
events.text: No.

That silhouette—she knew it. She'd seen it in the slope corridor. That's the back of someone who keeps everything bottled at the deepest level, someone accustomed to standing in front, to replacing feeling with calculation. Her teammate. Someone she knew. That person now stood there, back turned, hand on a terminal that if pressed couldn't be taken back.

events.speaker: Lyra
events.text: I know why you want to do this. I know what you're thinking, I know your calculation, I know in your logic this is the only plan that completely solves the problem. I know all of this, Soren, so I'm not here to tell you you're wrong.
Only then did he turn around and look at her. Something in his eyes—what he'd always hidden under that calm—was showing now because it was too heavy for the calm to cover anymore.

events.speaker: Soren
events.text: Then why are you here?

events.speaker: Lyra
events.text: To stop you. I can't let you do this.

events.speaker: Soren
events.text: You can't stop me, Lyra. You know it. Your tactical judgment, your sync rate, your weapon output—at every level, you can't beat me here.

events.speaker: Lyra
events.text: I know. But I still have to try.
She connected her weapon module.

Soren stepped away from the terminal, moving into combat stance. The control room wasn't large—less than ten meters between them. He watched her take position after connecting her module, and his eyes paused for a moment——her stance had changed. Not technically—something more fundamental. The synchronization state that in his memory was always somewhat strained under pressure now was deep, stable, grounded.
Then they both moved.

Her first move came half a beat faster than he predicted. Not speed—precision. Her weapon output landed exactly at the thinnest point in his defense arc, an angle he hadn't expected her to find. He sidestepped, pressed, reassessed——not chance. Her second move landed at another point requiring his attention.
Not chance.

He accelerated. His predictions were accurate, his responses to every move calculated, but this time he actually had to respond. Not easy suppression—a real confrontation in this control room. Her synchronization depth meant every output had no waste on error. Accurate, clean, each strike landed exactly where it needed.

She took the first impact, staggered two steps, regained balance. That impact was easier for her neural connection to absorb than before, like that deep space was also buffering external shock. She clenched her teeth, recalibrated, continued. Her sync consumption was slower than any past combat.

She took the second hit on her right arm. The module's connection shook, output dropped. She stopped for half a second, recalibrated—that recalibration was faster than she expected, the connection reestablishing more quickly.
Soren didn't speak, but his eyes had something when she sent her third strike precisely into his defense gap—something he'd never seen in her before. He paused half a beat.
In that half-beat, she surged forward, her weapon at close range, pushing him a step away from the terminal.

events.speaker: Soren
events.text: Your synchronization is different than before.

She didn't answer, relaunching her attack. Her deep synchronization made this confrontation last longer than his original calculation, each output without waste, each defense more stable than he expected. But his tactical judgment and accumulated combat experience created a real gap. Over time, that gap began to widen. Her sync consumption accelerated, her movements began showing slight delay.

Elias was on the control room's side. He wasn't joining the fight—he was doing something else, trying to access the main terminal from the side, attempting to find a way to pause or delay that directive. Soren's lock architecture displayed in the system—tight, nearly seamless. He searched the seams, searching while listening to the battle behind him.

Lyra was shoved back hard. She hit the control room's side wall, the metal giving a dull sound on impact, and she slid down, supporting herself against the wall, trying to stand. Her legs had no strength. Her sync rate was at a very low number, her body's response slowing.

Soren walked in front of her and stood, looking down at her. His expression—she'd never seen him like this. The calm exterior that always kept everything pressed deepest inside was completely gone now. What showed was something that made your heart ache looking at it, too heavy, too deep, like a container filled with too much, every crack seeping.

events.speaker: Soren
events.text: You can't stand anymore. Lyra, you lost. Move.
She supported herself against the wall and slowly stood. Her legs trembled. She got steady, looked up at him.

events.speaker: Lyra
events.text: Not yet.
